title: How PL R&D accelerates computing breakthroughs
summary: The first in a series: how PL R&D moves frontier ideas from open research into deployed systems across four focus areas — and how we drive, measure, and sustain breakthroughs.
html:
For discussion

Why we exist and what we do

Computing is reshaping various dimensions of human agency. PL R&D exists to expand and safeguard human freedom, coordination, intelligence, and cognition by accelerating computing breakthroughs.

Our R&D pipeline aims to move frontier ideas from open research through productionizing and into deployed systems that scale globally – each stage compounding on the last.

Our work spans four focus areas:

  • Digital Human Rights (freedom): the digital foundations that fundamental rights now depend on
  • Programmable Economies & Governance (coordination): the open infrastructure that enables coordination among states, institutions, and individuals
  • Safe AI & Robotics (intelligence): the frontier capabilities that keep machine intelligence under human direction
  • Neurotechnology (cognition): the interface that lets human cognition expand by connecting brains with the rest of computing

We break down each frontier field into no more than four opportunity spaces: tight clusters of technology and practice that often share expert networks, infrastructure, and bottlenecks.

Each opportunity space, in turn, names a small number of inflection points: specific, observable, falsifiable events whose arrival would tell us a field has irreversibly crossed over. We think of an inflection point as the moment when a capability becomes the default, when a market crosses a threshold of trust, and when a regulator can no longer pretend a technology does not exist.

How we drive breakthroughs

Today, much frontier work is too early, too interdisciplinary, or too radical for traditional capital structures to support alone. Additionally, even if money is available a binding constraint is often coordination. Our role is to create the conditions for discovery rather than discovery directly ourselves: we help researchers, builders, funders, and institutions coordinate around technical primitives, open infrastructure, and deployment pathways.

Each focus area is stewarded by a Focus Area Lead and has its own Scientific Advisory Board, a standing group of researchers and practitioners. Together, they map the field, define the opportunity spaces, name the inflection points within reach, and identify the bottlenecks blocking them.

Once the bottlenecks are named, the toolkit that releases them is plural by design, because different bottlenecks respond to different interventions:

  • Field-building communications (e.g., blog posts, whitepapers, roadmaps, taxonomies, websites, regulatory & policy thought pieces), when the bottleneck is legibility both to field-internal and external audiences: a field that lacks a shared map and naming conventions is difficult to coordinate and recruit for; written artifacts are a precondition for turning a loose collection of teams, researchers, and funders into a field with sustained momentum.
  • Convenings (e.g., dinners, retreats, residencies, hackathons), when the bottleneck is too few connections. Progress often hinges on getting the right people in the same room at the right time: a single dinner that seats a national census team across from a team building broad-listening tools (software for surfacing what large populations actually think) can produce a pilot, then a tool, then adoption at scale.
  • Grants and prizes (e.g., open RFPs, retroactive funding, breakthrough prizes), when the bottleneck is funding and other resources (incl. ideas): pre-commercial work that no for-profit will reach in time needs capital that does not demand a near-term return; we run our own programs and help peer funders deploy theirs, pairing our field-mapping with their capital scale.
  • Venture conversion (e.g., spinouts, founder matching, introductions to PL Capital, pilot projects), when the bottleneck is no longer research but translation into (commercial) existence: when the work is ready to leave the lab, we help teams find the capital, talent, and discipline that only a company can provide and/or deploy pilot projects.
  • Policy, standards & regulatory engagement (e.g., participation in standards bodies, regulatory sandboxes, procurement-pathway and model-framework design, agency and multilateral partnerships), when the bottleneck is that the rules do not yet permit, recognize, or know how to engage with new technology: we work inside the rule-making processes themselves to turn "not allowed yet" into a pathway others can follow.
  • Other: tbd

How we measure our success

Three questions will measure the success of our work over the coming years:

  1. Did the inflection points happen?
  2. Did they matter?
  3. Did our work meaningfully make them/other progress happen?

We are committed to publishing our progress.

How frontier R&D actually happens is, to us, itself an open research question. That is why we stay deliberately broad about what counts as a useful intervention and will expand our toolkit as we learn. Over time, we expect to produce breakthroughs not only across the four focus areas but in how R&D itself is done.

How we are structured is deliberately different from other organizations aiming to drive advances across these fields:

  • A corporate R&D function attached to a large engineering org (GoogleX) has internal deployment channels and manufacturing capacity behind its bets. We have neither, but also no corporate reporting lines that distort what we fund.
  • Government agencies like DARPA or ARIA hold procurement power and a funder-as-buyer model we lack. We compensate with speed and speculative range.
  • A family office deploys capital from a principal's balance sheet and measures by financial return. We operate at a field level and measure by outcomes that no single funder controls.

We recognize the following challenges:

  • Overextension: it may be too difficult to go deep enough in any single of the four focus areas to actually move bottlenecks.
  • Compounding delay: venture conversions generating PLC returns that fund the next generation of R&D will take many years.
  • Tool:bottleneck mismatch: defaulting to familiar interventions rather than ones that match a specific diagnosis.
  • Attribution failure: we cannot improve the model if we cannot measure what works.
  • Lost in noise: we may produce good work but could still fail to be heard. Reaching the right people needs to be one of our core competencies.

How we sustain momentum

A field-building organization that runs entirely on philanthropic capital is structurally fragile and prone to caution. That is why PL R&D sits inside the broader Protocol Labs network, where the model is designed to compound: PL R&D's field-building de-risks frontier areas; ventures emerge from those fields supported by PLVS; PL Capital invests with unusual early access and conviction; and when those ventures compound, a portion of the returns funds the next round of R&D. Initially, most R&D funding comes from grants and co-funders. But the model is built to compound, and every focus area is chosen partly on whether it can plausibly feed that loop.

Our strengths lie in turning frontier ideas into open technical primitives (vs. "just" grantmaking), covering the full builder pipeline from idea to company to ecosystem, routing capital at scale beyond our own balance sheet, working credibly at the seams between fields, and building better funding infrastructure.

What comes next

This article is the first in a series. The four focus-area posts that follow are themselves an exercise of the field-building communications described above. Each goes deep on one focus area: the opportunity spaces within it, the specific inflection points we think are within reach in the next five years, and the teams already pushing the frontiers.

title: Announcing the Juan Benet Podcast: Launching With First Guest Max Hodak, CEO of Science Corp
summary: Juan Benet Podcast launches with Max Hodak on neurotech, brain interfaces, Fast R&D, and more.

title: Data Sovereignty in a Privacy-First Future
summary: Builders redefining privacy and sovereignty — where control of data, identity, and computation is real, not just promised. Highlights from the teams working at the frontier of digital human rights.

title: Can Attestation Follow in the Footsteps of TLS?
summary: Attestation could follow TLS's path: standardize trust signals, clarify roles, and mature tooling. A look at what it would take for remote attestation to become infrastructure-grade.

title: Threads From the 2025 Cypherpunk Retreat
summary: Key takeaways from the Cypherpunk Retreat, where 100 leading builders advanced privacy-tech coordination. Themes included TEEs, MPC, and the next generation of private computation.

title: BitRobot and the Future of Embodied AI
summary: Exploring how crypto incentives and decentralized infrastructure can accelerate embodied AI and robotics — and why open coordination matters for the next wave of physical AI.

title: Driving Breakthroughs: The Impact of Our Innovation Network
summary: Reflecting on Protocol Labs' 2024 impact across neurotech, digital human rights, AI, and economies — and the vision for 2025 and beyond.

title: The Transformative Power of Neurotechnology
summary: A deep dive into neurotechnology's potential to reshape treatment of brain disorders, augment human cognition, and accelerate our understanding of the brain. Why Protocol Labs is investing in this frontier.

title: PL Vision: Driving Breakthroughs in Computing to Push Humanity Forward
summary: At a crossroads of technological capability and institutional inadequacy, Protocol Labs articulates its mission: building the infrastructure for a more open, equitable, and resilient internet.
